lesson.menu_title: Present Perfect -- The Story of a 41-Year Career
lesson.menu_desc: Telling your whole career with present perfect: for, since, ever, just, already, yet -- 34 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 2 -- Present Perfect as an Executive Tool
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucia Nishiyama Serra | Lesson 2 -- Present Perfect as an Executive Tool

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
the period of time you hold a job or an important position
b
an important point or achievement in a journey or a career
c
to lead an important new project or effort
d
a person who is among the first to do or develop something
e
to move from one role, stage or situation to another
f
to succeed in doing something difficult after real effort
g
to find your way through a difficult or complex situation
h
the lasting impact or contribution you leave behind
i
a complete and important change in something
j
to accept a new responsibility or challenge
k
to leave a role or situation and go to the next one
l
the history of your past achievements and results

reading.rtitle: Telling the Story of a Long Career
paras: A long career is a story, not a list. When you have worked for many years, a plain list of jobs and dates says very little. Recruiters in the United States do not only want the dates; they want to know what you have accomplished and what you have learned along the way. The present perfect is the tense that connects your past to your present -- it turns experience into a living story.

Think of a senior leader who has spent four decades in technology. She has led teams on three continents. She has navigated more than one crisis. She has spearheaded projects that changed her company. She has reached important milestones, and she has built a track record that speaks for itself. None of these sentences give an exact date, and that is the point: they describe experience that still matters today.

You do not need forty years to use this tool. Look at your own journey and ask: What have I accomplished? What challenges have I taken on? Where have I made a difference? Have I ever led a change that others said was impossible? When you answer with the present perfect -- 'I have built', 'I have led', 'I have delivered' -- your career stops sounding like a resume and starts sounding like a legacy.
reading.source: Alumni by Better -- B1 reading, adapted from a career column

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: The text explains how to write the perfect resume with exact dates for every job.
A long career is a story, and the present perfect turns your experience into something that still matters today.
The text says only people with forty years of experience are allowed to talk about their careers.

parts: Over the course of my career, I have
at six technology companies. I have
teams on three continents. I have
several important milestones, and I have just
in this field for over forty years.

gapfill.text: Present perfect = have / has + past participle. Use it for life experience (no exact date), for recent news with just / already / yet, and for duration with for and since. A finished past time (in 2010, last year) takes the past simple instead.

bank.label: Useful sentences to tell your career story
items: I have built teams across five continents.
I have spearheaded projects that transformed the business.
I have navigated my share of difficult moments.
I have been in technology for over forty years.
I have just taken on a new challenge in AI safety.
I have reached several milestones I am proud of.
I have led three major transformations.
I have never stopped learning something new.

items.situation: What is the most significant thing you have accomplished in your career?
tips: The thing I am most proud of is ...

items.situation: Have you ever led a team through a difficult moment?
How long have you worked in your field?
tips: I have worked in ... for over ...

items.situation: What new challenge have you taken on recently?
What is one milestone you have reached that means a lot to you?
tips: One milestone I have reached is ...

items: Round 1 -- Guided
In three sentences, describe your career so far. Use 'I have worked...', 'I have led...', and 'I have accomplished...'.
Round 2 -- The highlight reel
Talk about two or three milestones you have reached. Say why each one matters to you today.
Round 3 -- Ninety seconds
Give a ninety-second version of your whole career using the present perfect at least four times. No notes.
Curveball
A recruiter asks: 'Have you ever managed a project that failed?' Answer honestly, using the present perfect.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model career summary
list: Model: 'I have spent over forty years in technology. I have led teams on three continents, and I have spearheaded several major transformations. I have just moved into AI safety, which is the most exciting challenge I have ever taken on. Over the years, I have reached many milestones, but I have never stopped learning.'
Experience (no date): I have led global teams. / Have you ever worked abroad?
Recent and relevant now: I have just joined a new team. / I have already started.
Duration: I have worked here FOR ten years. / I have been a leader SINCE the early 2000s.
answerkey.note: Present perfect = have / has + past participle. Use it for experience, recent news, and duration with for / since. Do not add an exact finished time -- 'I led a team in 2010' is past simple, not present perfect.

listenings.text: People often ask me to summarize my career, and I always smile, because forty years is hard to fit into one minute. I have worked at some of the biggest names in technology. I have led engineering teams, I have spearheaded new products, and I have navigated more crises than I can count. I have reached milestones I never dreamed of when I started. But if you ask me what I am most proud of, it is not a title or a company. It is the people. I have mentored dozens of young engineers, and many of them have become leaders themselves. That, for me, is the real legacy.

Let me give you one piece of advice for your next interview in the United States. When they ask about your experience, do not give them a list of dates. Give them a story. Use the present perfect. Say I have led, I have built, I have delivered. These words tell the recruiter that your experience is alive and still matters today. I have coached many senior leaders, and the ones who get the offer are not always the ones with the most years. They are the ones who have learned to tell their story with confidence. So before your interview, write down five things you have accomplished. Practice them out loud until they feel natural. You have already done the work. Now learn to say it.

extra_audio.text: I have worked in technology for over forty years. I have led large teams on three continents. I have spearheaded several major transformations. I have just moved into the field of AI safety. It is the most exciting challenge I have ever taken on.
